translations.translation:
Hua He (Yongxian)
華覈 (永先)
Lived AD 219–278
aSanguozhiBiographyRecords of the Three Kingdoms [三國志]
byZL181
onSeptember 2nd, 2025
No History Reference defined for Officer ID '251' Hua He (Yongxian). Biography cannot be tied to a scroll in the Sanguozhi.

Hua He, styledYongxian, was fromWujin[武進],Wu commandery[吳郡].
He first served asCommandantofShangyu[上虞尉], thenCommandant who Organizes Agriculture. Due to his literary abilities, he entered [the central government] asGentlemen of Confidential Bureauand was promoted toDeputy of the Central Depository.
WhenShuwas annexed byWei,[Hua] Hememorialized this petition to the palace gates:
“I recently heard that thearmy of the traitorsswarmed thewest lands. Because thewest landsare of arduous terrain, I thought there should have been no worries. However, I just heard about the memorial ofLu Kangthat came, mentioning thatChengdu[成都] was not defended, that their subjects and theirlordwere displaced, and that theirAltars of Statewere destroyed. In the past, when Wei [衛] was being destroyed by the Di barbarians, theConqueringDuke[of Qi]preserved it.1But now in the present, with the lengthy and far distance, we cannot rescue. We have lost a land that was subservient to us and given up a nation that paid us tribute. Even as I am but a lowly official, I feel uneased.Your Majesty, you are wise and benevolent, and your vast grace comforts those afar. Suddenly hearing things like this, I am feeling sorrowful. I cannot contain my melancholy any further, and so I earnestly memorialize to informyou.”•••
In the year 660 BCE, a devastating attack on Wei [衛] by the Di people almost destroyed the declining state, but theConqueringDukeof Qihelped push back the invaders and kept the state of Wei [衛] alive.↩
AfterSun Haoassumed the throne, he was enfeoffed asDistrict Marquisof Xuling [徐陵亭侯]. In the second year of theera [267],[Sun] Haowas going to construct a new palace. It was mandatory that it would be vast and expansive, decorated with pearls and jade. The cost was exorbitant. In the middle of the summer, it was being constructed. Agriculture and defenses were both abandoned.[Hua] Hepetitioned to protest, stating:
“This minister of yours heard that during the reign of theCivil[Emperor] ofHan, theNine Provinces[九州] were at peace. The people ofQinwere glad to be rid of the cruelty and harshness of a tyrannical government, and they submitted to the lenience and benevolence of theLiu clan. Labor was reduced and laws were simplified, ushering in rejuvenated governance. Royal sons and younger brothers were given their own fiefdoms to protect theHan dynasty. At that time, everyone believed that it was secure asMount Tai[泰山] and that it had a boundless foundation. However, it was only Jia Yi [賈誼] who believed there were three issues that made him cry bitterly and shed tears, and six issues that made him heave long sighs. He asked how the situation at the time could be any different than one embracing fire by stacking firewood and sleeping on top of it, then claiming to be safe as the fire had not started. The chaos that followed went exactly what he said.2Although I am lowly and foolish, being unable to have understanding in overall logic, I believe that past matters can be used to conjecture the present situation. [Jia] Yi [賈誼] had said, ‘After a few years, the feudal princes will want to be more powerful. They will dismiss and send back tutors and chancellors fromHanwith the excuse that they were ill, wanting to do that so that they could govern [alone]. Even if there wereYaoandShun, they could not bring stability!’ And now, ourgreat foeoccupies the lands of theNine Provinces[九州], possesses the majority of the population, has proficiency in many tactics of warfare, and is taking advantage of the weaponry and horses [accumulated by the] former powers. If we want to contend with them, the central state, in annexing one another, it would be like the situation where Chu andHancould not coexist, not merely the one of thePrinceofHuainan[淮南王] and thePrinceofJibei[濟北王] duringHan.3The things that [Jia] Yi [賈誼] would have cried bitterly over is trivial compared to the present. The allegory of embracing fire by sleeping on firewood is more urgent now!
“TheGreat August Emperorobserved that the past was like that and saw that the present was like this, so he expanded agriculture and sericulture programs, thus accumulating uncountable reserves. He showed empathy to the people over their heavy labor and strived hard to train warriors for battle. Thus, everyone felt grateful and every individual was thinking about devoting themselves. His destiny had not been achieved when he left behind his vast empire too early. After that, powerful ministers usurped the government. They contradicted the will of Heaven and they violated popular sentiment, neglecting the basis of security and survival and pursuing the opportunity of a fleeting moment. They started military expeditions several times, exhausting the repositories and treasuries, tiring the soldiers, and straining the people. There was no moment where security was secured. Those who survive now are the remaining injured troops and the many sorrowful people. This has caused our military warehouses to be empty and our food depositories to be lacking. The handouts of clothes and silk cannot fulfill everything during the cold or hot seasons, and in addition, many have lost their jobs, so families and households cannot support themselves. However, thenorthhas stored their grain to support their people, letting them focus entirely on the east without further concerns.Shuserved as a western vassal state and its lands were arduous and secure. In addition, it had inherited the way of ruling from theFormer Lord, so it was thought that its defense would be enough to last a long time. Never it had been anticipated that it would suddenly collapse one morning! Now that the lips are gone, the teeth will be cold; [such a situation] is something the ancients were worried about. The commanderies ofJiao province[交州] are the southern lands of our empire, but now with bothJiaozhi[交阯郡] andJiuzhen[九真郡] commanderies fallen,Rinan[日南] is isolated and in danger, and its survival to hard to protect. Northward ofHepu[合浦], the people are all shaken up, and so they continuously evade military service, and there are many desertions and mutinies. The garrisons have been reduced and our mighty defenses have become puny. I often fear that at a single moment, unexpected changes will occur. In the past, maritime brigands were coveting the eastern counties, and they received many deserting people who were familiar with navigation on sea. They are more greedy than they were in past years, and they plunder and rob every day. And now, there are enemies in the front and in the back, and there are many troubles from head to tail. This is a dangerous period for our empire.
“It is sincerely advisable to cease construction and preemptively devise defensive plans, and to incentivize the reclamation and cultivation of wasteland in order to provide relief to the hungry. I only fear that the harvest period will elapse soon and that the spring farming is nearing its end, so if an emergency happens one day, we will not be completely prepared. If we forsake these priorities and devote all our strength to construction, and something difficult and unexpected suddenly arises, we would have to abandon ramming earth, respond to the signal fires and beacon fires, urge the resentful and bitter soldiers, and face the gleaming blades. This is something our great foe can exploit for their own gain. If we instead have sturdy defenses, that will stretch out the days and lengthen the wait, thereby ensuring that their military provisions will be depleted. There will be no need for blades to strike each other, as their warriors will betired.•••
Jia Yi [賈誼] wanted a strong central government, observing that the local princes were becoming more unruly. The rebellion of the seven princedoms occurred not long after his petition, destabilizing the empire.↩
These two princes were executed promptly after their intent to rebel was learned by the Han court.↩
“During the reign of Tai Wu [太戊] of yore, mulberry trees and millets grew in his court. He was so afraid that he cultivated virtue. They strangely withered away andYinprospered.4When the Shimmering Planet [Mars] stayed in the Heart constellation, Song [宋] believed it to be disastrous. After theReveredDuke[景公] consulted the words of the astronomers, the Shimmering Planet moved away and the reign of theReveredDukelengthened.5If one cultivates virtue within themselves, they can impress different ethnicities and the words they speak out of their mouth can reach the gods. Because of my foolishness and obliviousness, I had erred and disgraced my rank of a close minister, and I was unable to help show your benevolence and grace to impress the deities. Looking up [at my superiors], I feel ashamed, and gazing down [at my inferiors], I feel disgraced, having nowhere where I could go. I withdrew and went into hiding to contemplate deeply. I speculate that the signs like the Shimmering Planet and the mulberry trees and millets that were shown to those two lords are by Heaven itself, with other minor phenomena almost certainly being the doing of door gods and lesser deities. I have checked with Heaven and Earth that there were no other ominous occurrences. Instead, there were many auspicious omens and lucky signs that repeatedly came one after another: a resplendent pearl was seen and a white sparrow was next witnessed. Such boundless blessings were truly sent out by spirits! They regard the Nine Provinces [九域] as their homes and the realm under heaven as their household. They are not the same as the people of registered households who move around. The current palace was built by theformer emperor. The land was divined before the foundation was built, and it was without ill portents. Moreover, the markets of Yang [楊] and the lands there are connected to the palace. If the grand project is completed, the imperial chariot will move there and all the door gods will have to transfer. I fear that it will not be superior to the old [palace] for a very long time. Constant moves cannot be done, but if you stay, you will be disgruntled. This is why I am anxious, day and night.
“I have examined theLaws of Months, and it said that in the waning month of summer, there can be no work in earth, no assembly of feudal lords, and no military operations that excite the populace, that if a major project went forward, there would certainly be a great misfortune. Though there may be no assembly of feudal lords at this present moment, the army of a feudal lord is no different than an assembly. During the Wu and Ji heavenly stems in the sixth month, the virtue of the earth flourishes, and it cannot be offended; in addition, there is also the harvest month, and it cannot be missed during its time. In antiquity, when the Temporary Duke of Lu [魯隱公] built Zhongqiu [中丘] during the summer, theChroniclesrecorded it so as to pass it onto posterity as a warning. To build a palace now would serve as a great foundation for many generations, but violate the great taboo set by Heaven and Earth. If you follow what theChroniclesrecorded, you would be abandoning the important task respectfully bestowed [by Heaven]. In my humble opinion, this makes me uneased. I also fear that if we summon the people who avoid, some of them will not come. If we attack them, we would have to abandon labor and start a conflict, and if we do not attack them, then they will become more insolent with each day and month. Supposing if all of them came, there would be a gathering of a large crowd, and it would be unlikely none of them fell ill. Moreover, when the hearts of people are eased, they think about doing goodwill, but if they are bitter, they think about betraying. Our elite troops south of theYangzi Riverare difficult for the northern soldiers to handle, who will want to muster ten soldiers for each one of us. The realm has not been stabilized yet, and so I am deeply worried about this. When the palace is finished, then if the number of those who died or betrayed is five thousand, the northern army will increase by fifty thousand, and if that number reaches ten thousand, then [the increase] will be doubled to one hundred thousand. The sick will suffer from dying while traitors will pass on unfavorable words, which will bring elation to our great foe. Now if we are going to struggle with the central plains to decide who is strong and who is weak, then at this present moment, they are becoming stronger and we are becoming weaker, and in addition, we are exhausted. This is something mighty heroes and wise men are deeply anxious about.
“I heard that when past kings governed their state without three years worth of reserves, it was said that their state could not be a state. In an era of calm and peace, we need to be prepared in such a manner, more so when our foe is mighty and grand, and we have ignored agriculture and neglected to accumulate [reserves]. Although we have planted and cultivated crops now, they have been submerged by floods. Those that survived have to be weeded and harvested, but the senior local officials are so afraid about the schedule that in high-lying commanderies, they personally go to the hills and forests there to devote all their strength into chopping wood into timber, thus abandoning agriculture and neglecting their duties. The wives and children of officials and peasants are weak and young, and when the cultivated land is meager, then if there is a flood or drought, there will be nothing they can get, forever. The rice in provinces and commanderies should be reserved until an emergency comes, but there are people that eat redundantly who look towards the government for charity. If every location is deprived and empty, then the land and naval transportation routes cannot supply, and when the northern foe invades our frontier, even if [Duke of] Zhou [周公] and [Duke of] Shao [召公] were reborn6and[Zhang] Liangand[Chen] Pingemerged again, they would be unable to formulate a clear strategy forYour Majesty. I heard that when the sovereign is wise, the subjects are loyal, and when the sovereign is sagacious, the subjects are candid. As such, I respectfully venture to offend your divine might, and I beg you to closely examinethis.”•••
During the Shang dynasty, the ruler Tai Wu installed Yi Zhi [伊陟] as his chancellor. In Bo [亳], the capital, auspicious mulberry trees and millets grew together in the court, becoming large and thick in one evening. The ruler Tai Wu was afraid and asked Yi Zhi about it. Yi Zhi said, “I heard that evil cannot triumph over virtue. Might your governance have some flaws in it? Your Majesty would do well to improve your virtue.” Tai Wu followed this, and the auspicious mulberry trees and millets died and went away.↩
Zi Luan [子栾] was the ruler of the State of Song. Once, Mars went into the Heart [心] Mansion, which was the zone of Song. A zone is the astrological belief that certain lands on earth corresponded with certain zones in the heavens. Anything that happened in the zones of heavens, such as comets, supernovas, and other astronomical occurrences, was said to give clues to the future of the lands on earth. In this case, the ministers interpreted Mars being in the Heart Mansion as a herald of disaster. However, Zi Luan rejected attributing it to his chancellor, his people, and the years, saying that he should rely on his chancellor, depend on his people, and that if the years were not good, he would not be king. These were considered virtuous, and there was no disaster.↩
Traditional writings state that the Duke of Zhou and the Duke of Shao upheld the realm during the Gonghe Regency, a period of relative prosperity and stability.↩
This petition was memorialized, but[Sun] Haodid not accept it.
He was later promoted toDirector of the Eastern Towerand becameRight National Historian. When[Hua] Hepetitioned to decline,[Sun] Haoreplied, “I have obtained your petition: ‘The Eastern Tower is an office where Ruist scholars gather like a forest to proofread literature and judge doubts and difficulties. Those who were given such posts during the time ofHanwere all renowned and learned major scholars. I thus beg to be replaced with someone outstanding and worthy.’ Hearing this, I thought that because you had extensive knowledge on thefenanddianclassics, as well as being rich in knowledge and diverse in information, you could be said to be someone who enjoys rites and music, and values odes and books. You should show off your literary skills through swift strokes to help with current affairs. In this way, you would surpass the likes ofYang [Xiong], Ban [Gu] [班固], Zhang [Heng] [張衡], andCai [Yong].7I was bewildered, then, that you are of radiant humility, belittling yourself in an extreme manner. You should strive to manage your duties and surpass the past sages. Do not give me any furtherbabble!”•••
These four men were notable writers, scholars, poets, and calligraphers.↩
At the time, there were no reserves in the food depositories, yet the affairs of the realm were becoming more extravagant and luxurious.[Hua] Hepetitioned:
“Presently, thedastardly caitiffsare wealthy, and their conquests are not finished yet. We lack reserves of accumulated years in our warehouses, and we lack provisions we can meet our foes with in our campaigns. This is thus something that should be a grave concern to a ruler. The means of producing wealth and food all stem from the people. To seize the time of the seasons to focus on agriculture is a paramount urgency of a state. Yet, the various bureaus in the capital, despite differences in what they administer, each issue requisitions. They do not care for amount of manpower and always give the closest deadlines. Senior officials dread punishment and therefore urge the people day and night to abandon their farmland work and hasten before the deadline day. When they arrive at the capital, accumulated goods are occasionally not even used. This has only caused the common people to exert less and miss the time of the seasons. In the harvesting months during autumn, we supervise their dues. We deprive them of time to sow and grow, and yet we demand their taxes for the year. If they have arrears, then we register their property and confiscate them, along with their wealth and goods. Thus, families and households are poor and desperate, their clothes and food insufficient. We should temporarily cease military service and focus our hearts on agriculture and sericulture. The ancients have said, ‘If a man does not plow, he will likely suffer from hunger. If a woman does not weave, she will likely suffer from coldness.’ Thus, when past kings ruled their states, they would focus on agriculture.
“Ever since militaries rose up, a hundred years or so have already elapsed. The farmers have abandoned the work of cultivating fields8and the female workers have stopped the business of using looms. We can estimate based off this that cases where people eat vegetarian meals and starve for long periods or wear thin clothes and walk on ice are not uncommon. I have heard that there are two things that a lord seeks from his people. I have also heard that there are three things that there are three things that the people desire from their lord. The two wants are the desire for others to toil for him and the desire for others to die for him. The three expectations are that those who are starved can eat, that those who are toiled can rest, and that those who are accomplished can be rewarded. If the people delivers on those two things, but their lord skips their three expectations, then resentment will breed and accomplishments will not be fulfilled. As of present, the national treasuries are not filled up and the labor of the people is overwhelming. The two wants of the lord are already fulfilled, but the three expectations of the people have not been repaid. What is more, a starving person will not wait for appealing delicacies to later satiate themselves and a cold person will not wait for fox or raccoon-dog pelts to later warm themselves. Tastiness is but an extraordinary sensation for the mouth and intricate embroidery are but adornments on a body.
“Right now, there are a lot of problems, the labor is complex, and the common people are poor, their customs extravagant. Yet, craftsmen produce tools of no use while women make impressive and extravagant adornments. They are not diligent in growing hemp,9but focus on exquisite fabrics and ornate patterns, mimicking each other and being ashamed when they are the only ones who lack such. The families of soldiers and people also pursue these vulgar customs, being without reserves of a few grain within their homes, and yet having dresses of exquisite silk for going outside. As for the families of rich businessmen and trade merchants, they are most extravagant and indulgent, all the more by having gold and silver. Right now, the world is not yet at peace and the common people are lacking in supplies. We should devote ourselves to the sources of supporting the people and enriching the industries of grain and silk. Instead, we shirk work to superficial and ostentatious trends and we waste days on luxurious and extravagant work. There is thus no distinction between superior and inferior ranks and we would have the damage of squandering money and wasting force.
“These days, families of officials and soldiers are rarely without children. In most cases, they have three to four while in smaller cases, they have one to two. We should give orders to households to have one daughter. One hundred thousand families would have one hundred thousand women. If women can weave and spin one bolts in one year, we will have one hundred thousand bolts. If those within the four borders were of the same heart and joined forces, then within several years, cloth and silk would surely be accumulated.
“We should permit the people to wear the five colors. The only prohibition would be on the adornments of exquisite silk that have no benefit. Moreover, those who have attractive looks do not need to wait for extravagant hues to increase their good looks and those who have gorgeous appearances do not need to wait for fine silk to attract adoration. The adornments of the five colors are enough beauty. If one exhausts white powder and black eyebrow makeup and uses up all the fancy dresses, ugly woman will not necessarily disappear. If one abolishes extravagant hues and does away with intricate embroidery, beautiful people will not necessarily disappear. If this theory is true, then if there is no benefit in having them and no harm in abolishing them, why cherish them and not temporarily prohibit them so as to fill the warehouses, a more urgent situation? This is the paramount method of alleviating the needy and the foundational basis of enriching the nation. Even ifGuan [Yiwu]and Yan [Ying] [晏嬰] came back to life, there would be nothing for them to change.
“TheCivil[Emperor]and theRevered[Emperor] ofHanhad generational peace between successions. The world was at peace with no troubles from the four directions, but they still believed that carved inscriptions hindered agricultural work and brocaded embroideries harmed female crafts. They thus inaugurated the benefits of an enriched nation and prevented the roots of hunger and coldness. Today, the six directions are all divided, dholes and wolves fill the streets, armies have not left the borders, and armor has not been ungirdled. Will we not increase the sources of producing wealth and fill the reserves ofwarehouses?”•••
Here, “cultivating fields” [南畝] literally means “southern fields”; this is because the fields facing the sun southward grew better.↩
Hemp was a common material for making clothes.↩
[Sun] Hao, considering that[Hua] Hewas elderly in age, commanded him to draft a petition, and when[Hua] Hedid not dare do it, he commanded him again to draft a text, standing next to him and waiting.[Hua] Hewrote this text:
“Alas, I,[Hua] He, am but a petty official, trivial as grass and trash!
“I was blessed to be valued by thesovereign, receiving grace and special favor.
“It is as if I arose from rotten soil and molted anew in the court.
“Ah, the resplendent purple palace gates! The flourishing green chain-link patterns!
“And with the gushing clear dew, I bathed in the blissful breeze.
“My merits are not even worth tiny yak hair, but my faults are as lofty as mountains.
“But the moist cleaned off the dirt; your grace and leniency was extremely generous!
“The filthy are honored, their petty lives lengthened.
“I wanted to repay the boundless [favor] and so I devoted myself to the august sky.
“The grace ofYour Sagacious and Graceful Majestyis like pouring rain, dismissing my fault!
“That when I failed to provide an answer, [gentle] moist covered me!
“I do not dare disobey a command, for I fear that I will sin and be executed.
“Being bold to accept an imperial order, my spirit has passed, but my body lives on!”
All of the explanations of what to do according to a situation, recommendations of the virtuous, and removals of the guilty that[Hua] Hemade over time numbered over one hundred petitions. They were all helpful, but as the words were too much, they were not recorded in whole. In the first year of theera [275], he was removed due to a minor crime. Several years later, he died. The discussions, works, letters, and petitions of[Wei] Yaoand[Hua] Hehave all been passed onto this generation.
